panel.origin: No sharps and no flats έΑΦ the only major key written on entirely white piano keys. This is the reference point at the top (12 o'clock) of the circle.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: A minor (shares the exact same notes). Parallel minor: C minor (same tonic, three flats). A minor is the most important relationship έΑΦ same key signature, different home note.
panel.mixology: I = C ┬╖ ii = Dm ┬╖ iii = Em ┬╖ IV = F ┬╖ V = G ┬╖ vi = Am ┬╖ vii┬░ = B diminished. The three you'll use most are C, F, and G (IέΑΥIVέΑΥV).
panel.examples: Bright, plain, and neutral έΑΦ the blank canvas of music. Countless pop and folk songs live here because it's the easiest key to read and play. 'Let It Be' and endless beginner pieces sit in C.

panel.origin: One sharp: Fέβψ. The first step clockwise from C. To find a key's sharps, the last sharp is always one half-step below the key name (Fέβψ έΗΤ G).
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: E minor. Parallel minor: G minor (two flats). E minor is one of the most common keys in guitar music, so G major and E minor turn up everywhere together.
panel.mixology: I = G ┬╖ ii = Am ┬╖ iii = Bm ┬╖ IV = C ┬╖ V = D ┬╖ vi = Em ┬╖ vii┬░ = Fέβψ diminished.
panel.examples: Warm and open, and a favorite on guitar because the open strings ring out. A huge amount of folk, country, and rock lives in G έΑΦ it simply sounds good on a guitar.

panel.origin: Two sharps: Fέβψ and Cέβψ. Two steps clockwise. The sharps always added in the same order: Fέβψ then Cέβψ.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: B minor. Parallel minor: D minor (one flat). B minor gives D major a darker cousin much used in classical and folk.
panel.mixology: I = D ┬╖ ii = Em ┬╖ iii = Fέβψm ┬╖ IV = G ┬╖ V = A ┬╖ vi = Bm ┬╖ vii┬░ = Cέβψ diminished.
panel.examples: Bright, triumphant, and resonant on string instruments έΑΦ the violin's open strings reinforce it, so much joyful classical music (and Pachelbel's Canon) is in D.

panel.origin: Three sharps: Fέβψ, Cέβψ, Gέβψ. Three steps clockwise from C.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: Fέβψ minor. Parallel minor: A minor (no accidentals έΑΦ back to C's signature). Fέβψ minor is a richly expressive key in its own right.
panel.mixology: I = A ┬╖ ii = Bm ┬╖ iii = Cέβψm ┬╖ IV = D ┬╖ V = E ┬╖ vi = Fέβψm ┬╖ vii┬░ = Gέβψ diminished.
panel.examples: Confident and bright, comfortable for both guitar and strings. Common in rock and pop έΑΦ plenty of anthems sit in A for its punchy, open sound.

panel.origin: Four sharps: Fέβψ, Cέβψ, Gέβψ, Dέβψ. Four steps clockwise.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: Cέβψ minor. Parallel minor: E minor (one sharp). Cέβψ minor is a dramatic, much-loved key έΑΦ Beethoven's 'Moonlight' Sonata lives there.
panel.mixology: I = E ┬╖ ii = Fέβψm ┬╖ iii = Gέβψm ┬╖ IV = A ┬╖ V = B ┬╖ vi = Cέβψm ┬╖ vii┬░ = Dέβψ diminished.
panel.examples: Full and ringing on guitar (E is the lowest open string), so it's a backbone of blues and rock. Bright and powerful with real low-end resonance.

panel.origin: Five sharps: Fέβψ, Cέβψ, Gέβψ, Dέβψ, Aέβψ. Five steps clockwise έΑΦ getting toward the busy bottom of the circle.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: Gέβψ minor. Parallel minor: B minor (two sharps). B major is enharmonically equal to Cέβφ major (seven flats), the spelling you'd meet coming the other way.
panel.mixology: I = B ┬╖ ii = Cέβψm ┬╖ iii = Dέβψm ┬╖ IV = E ┬╖ V = Fέβψ ┬╖ vi = Gέβψm ┬╖ vii┬░ = Aέβψ diminished.
panel.examples: Brilliant and a little demanding to read with five sharps, so it's less common in beginner music but prized in jazz and for its bright, vivid color.

panel.origin: One flat: Bέβφ. The first step counter-clockwise from C. For flat keys, the second-to-last flat names the key έΑΦ except F, the one you simply memorize.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: D minor. Parallel minor: F minor (four flats). D minor is famously the 'saddest' key in popular lore.
panel.mixology: I = F ┬╖ ii = Gm ┬╖ iii = Am ┬╖ IV = Bέβφ ┬╖ V = C ┬╖ vi = Dm ┬╖ vii┬░ = E diminished.
panel.examples: Mellow, warm, and rounded. Common for woodwinds and a frequent home for ballads and folk songs έΑΦ softer-edged than the sharp keys.

panel.origin: Two flats: Bέβφ and Eέβφ. Two steps counter-clockwise. The second-to-last flat (Bέβφ) names the key.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: G minor. Parallel minor: Bέβφ minor (five flats). G minor is a deeply expressive key used across centuries of music.
panel.mixology: I = Bέβφ ┬╖ ii = Cm ┬╖ iii = Dm ┬╖ IV = Eέβφ ┬╖ V = F ┬╖ vi = Gm ┬╖ vii┬░ = A diminished.
panel.examples: Rich and resonant for brass and wind instruments έΑΦ many trumpets, clarinets, and saxophones are built in Bέβφ, so concert bands and jazz live here constantly.

panel.origin: Three flats: Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ. Three steps counter-clockwise. The second-to-last flat (Eέβφ) names the key.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: C minor. Parallel minor: Eέβφ minor (six flats). C minor is one of the great dramatic keys έΑΦ Beethoven's Fifth Symphony is in it.
panel.mixology: I = Eέβφ ┬╖ ii = Fm ┬╖ iii = Gm ┬╖ IV = Aέβφ ┬╖ V = Bέβφ ┬╖ vi = Cm ┬╖ vii┬░ = D diminished.
panel.examples: Warm, heroic, and noble έΑΦ long associated with grandeur (Beethoven's 'Eroica'). A cornerstone of band and jazz repertoire alongside Bέβφ.

panel.origin: Four flats: Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ, Dέβφ. Four steps counter-clockwise.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: F minor. Parallel minor: Aέβφ minor (seven flats). F minor is dark and intense, a frequent choice for somber music.
panel.mixology: I = Aέβφ ┬╖ ii = Bέβφm ┬╖ iii = Cm ┬╖ IV = Dέβφ ┬╖ V = Eέβφ ┬╖ vi = Fm ┬╖ vii┬░ = G diminished.
panel.examples: Lush, soft, and romantic έΑΦ a favorite for expressive piano writing. Chopin and the Romantics drew on its mellow, velvety character.

panel.origin: Five flats: Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ, Dέβφ, Gέβφ. Five steps counter-clockwise. Enharmonically equal to Cέβψ major (seven sharps).
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: Bέβφ minor. Parallel minor: Dέβφ minor (theoretical έΑΦ usually respelled as Cέβψ minor). Bέβφ minor is a rich, dark key well used in jazz and Romantic piano.
panel.mixology: I = Dέβφ ┬╖ ii = Eέβφm ┬╖ iii = Fm ┬╖ IV = Gέβφ ┬╖ V = Aέβφ ┬╖ vi = Bέβφm ┬╖ vii┬░ = C diminished.
panel.examples: Smooth, dark, and luxurious. It sits beautifully under the hands on piano (lots of black keys) and is a favorite for lush ballads and jazz standards.

panel.origin: Six flats: Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ, Dέβφ, Gέβφ, Cέβφ έΑΦ sitting at the bottom (6 o'clock) of the circle. This is the enharmonic twin of Fέβψ major (six sharps): the same sound, spelled two ways.
panel.chemistry: Relative minor: Eέβφ minor (or Dέβψ minor on the sharp side). Parallel: Gέβφ minor is theoretical. This is the point where the sharp road and the flat road meet at the same pitches.
panel.mixology: I = Gέβφ ┬╖ ii = Aέβφm ┬╖ iii = Bέβφm ┬╖ IV = Cέβφ ┬╖ V = Dέβφ ┬╖ vi = Eέβφm ┬╖ vii┬░ = F diminished. (Spell as Fέβψ major and it's Fέβψ, Gέβψm, Aέβψm, B, Cέβψ, Dέβψm, Eέβψ┬░ έΑΦ same keys, different names.)
panel.examples: Dark, rich, and exotic-sounding. Rare in beginner music because of all the accidentals, but loved by composers for its deep, shadowy color. Where the circle closes the loop.

THE NATURALS.en: The circle of fifths is named because each step clockwise jumps up a perfect fifth έΑΦ C to G to D and so on έΑΦ and after twelve fifths you land right back on C, closing the loop.
THE NATURALS.es: The circle of fifths is named because each step clockwise jumps up a perfect fifth έΑΦ C to G to D and so on έΑΦ and after twelve fifths you land right back on C, closing the loop.
THE NATURALS.fr: The circle of fifths is named because each step clockwise jumps up a perfect fifth έΑΦ C to G to D and so on έΑΦ and after twelve fifths you land right back on C, closing the loop.
THE NATURALS.it: The circle of fifths is named because each step clockwise jumps up a perfect fifth έΑΦ C to G to D and so on έΑΦ and after twelve fifths you land right back on C, closing the loop.

SHARP KEYS.en: There's a trick for sharp keys: the last sharp in the signature is always one half-step below the key's name. See an Fέβψ as the final sharp? The key is G. See a Gέβψ last? You're in A.
SHARP KEYS.es: There's a trick for sharp keys: the last sharp in the signature is always one half-step below the key's name. See an Fέβψ as the final sharp? The key is G. See a Gέβψ last? You're in A.
SHARP KEYS.fr: There's a trick for sharp keys: the last sharp in the signature is always one half-step below the key's name. See an Fέβψ as the final sharp? The key is G. See a Gέβψ last? You're in A.
SHARP KEYS.it: There's a trick for sharp keys: the last sharp in the signature is always one half-step below the key's name. See an Fέβψ as the final sharp? The key is G. See a Gέβψ last? You're in A.

FLAT KEYS.en: For flat keys (except F), the second-to-last flat names the key. Three flats έΑΦ Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ έΑΦ and the second-to-last is Eέβφ, so it's Eέβφ major. A handy shortcut once you spot it.
FLAT KEYS.es: For flat keys (except F), the second-to-last flat names the key. Three flats έΑΦ Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ έΑΦ and the second-to-last is Eέβφ, so it's Eέβφ major. A handy shortcut once you spot it.
FLAT KEYS.fr: For flat keys (except F), the second-to-last flat names the key. Three flats έΑΦ Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ έΑΦ and the second-to-last is Eέβφ, so it's Eέβφ major. A handy shortcut once you spot it.
FLAT KEYS.it: For flat keys (except F), the second-to-last flat names the key. Three flats έΑΦ Bέβφ, Eέβφ, Aέβφ έΑΦ and the second-to-last is Eέβφ, so it's Eέβφ major. A handy shortcut once you spot it.
